hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: Four well-dressed young African American women seated on the steps of a building at Atlanta University, circa 1899-1900
inline_images.caption: Four young women at Atlanta University, where Du Bois taught, photographed for his 1900 Paris Exposition exhibit; they embody the college-trained Black leadership his Talented Tenth essay calls on to uplift the race.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (African American Photographs Assembled for 1900 Paris Exposition; LCCN 95507126)
inline_images.license: Public domain (published in the United States before January 1, 1931)

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.why: Du Bois argues the college-trained few must lead and build the institutions that teach the rest, and Terry recounts building the W. E. B. Du Bois Department of Afro-American Studies from a program into a department.

related_chapters.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
related_chapters.chapter_title: It Does Not Have to Be This Way
related_chapters.why: Du Bois holds that the race is saved by developing its exceptional, highly educated few, and Hrabowski describes realizing the Meyerhoff Scholars Program, which produces Black PhDs in science at scale.

related_chapters.subject: Lonnie C. King
related_chapters.why: Du Bois casts the college-educated as the leaders of thought among their people, and King recounts the Atlanta University Center students having Roslyn Pope write the 1960 Appeal for Human Rights.

related_chapters.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
related_chapters.chapter_title: My Child Has to Learn How to Think
related_chapters.why: Du Bois insists the central task is training teachers, and teachers of teachers, and Glascoe's charge to the young is about teaching teachers to think.

related_chapters.subject: Amos C. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: Pass It On and Arriving at Morehouse
related_chapters.why: Du Bois names the Black colleges as the training ground of leaders, and Brown describes arriving at Morehouse, where he later studied under Martin Luther King Jr., and the charge to pass it on.

related_chapters.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton
related_chapters.why: Du Bois frames the educated leader's duty as teaching the mass through trained teachers, and Cotton describes the SCLC Citizenship Education teaching team of Septima Clark, Andrew Young, and herself that trained thousands of grassroots organizers.

top_voice.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
top_voice.reason: Terry moved from a Bennett College sit-in participant to building the W. E. B. Du Bois Department of Afro-American Studies, a direct enactment of Du Bois's call for college-trained leaders to build the institutions that teach the race.

closing_image.alt: Bennett College, Greensboro, North Carolina, 1910 archival photograph
closing_image.caption: Bennett College, Greensboro, North Carolina, 1910 archival photograph. Esther M. A. Terry is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.
closing_image.photographer: Unknown (W. N. Hartshorn and George W. Penniman, eds., institutional source book)

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions The Talented Tenth raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Myrtle Gonza Glascoe, C. T. Vivian, and John Churchville.
